nodes.title: First systematic null test (stellar density × lensmc bin1)
nodes.body:
Cross-spectra between Euclid tracer maps and systematic template maps, testing whether observational systematics leak into the shear signal. Produces 70 spectra: 5 systematics × 2 methods × 7 bins.

**Systematic maps.** Five VMPZ-ID templates at nside 16384 (NESTED): stellar density, galactic extinction, number of exposures, SAA passages, zodiacal light. Loaded via `dr1_cmbx.eDR1data.gctools.reader.read_partial_map()` which handles sparse FITS and downgrading to the analysis nside. Critical: HP UNSEEN sentinels (|val| > 1e20) must be zeroed after loading — they dominate the power spectrum if left in.

**Mask.** The analysis mask is the intersection of the survey footprint and the systematic map's coverage (non-zero pixels). Both apodized following the standard convention.

**Null hypothesis.** Under H0, the cross-spectrum C^{fS}_ℓ is consistent with zero — systematics should not correlate with shear. Knox chi2/dof ~ 1 for clean systematics. Galactic extinction is the exception: chi2/dof 1.3-9.0 across bins, with strongest contamination in high-z bins (5, 6). This is real astrophysical contamination, not a pipeline artifact.

**Downstream.** These spectra feed into the contamination metric (via `plot_contamination_metric`) and the summary heatmap (via `plot_systematic_null_tests`). The C^{SS} auto-spectrum (stored as `cl_sys_auto` in each NPZ) is needed for the full DES×SPT-style X metric.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/compute_systematic_cross_spectrum.py`

nodes.title: SACC combine uses Misc tracers — needs NZ upgrade
nodes.body:
Packages 28 individual cross-spectrum NPZ files into a single SACC file per CMB experiment, ready for downstream likelihood analysis. SACC (Save All Cross-Correlations) is the standard Euclid/LSST data vector format — the interface between spectrum estimation and parameter inference.

**Why SACC.** SACC files carry not just data vectors but also tracer metadata (n(z), beam functions), bandpower definitions, and covariance blocks. This is what the eDR1like likelihood reads. The format enables sharing spectra with the wider Euclid/LSST community and ensures that all information needed to compute theory predictions is bundled with the data.

**Tracers.** 14 shear tracers as NZTracer (z, n(z) from `RR2_v2_Nz_WL_C2020_sel_pv.fits`, 6 bins + "all" bin × 2 methods). 1 CMB κ tracer as MiscTracer (no n(z) — CMB lensing has a broad kernel computed internally by the theory code). Total: 15 tracers per file. Originally used MiscTracer for shear (with no n(z)), upgraded to NZTracer when likelihood integration required redshift distributions.

**Data vector.** 560 data points per SACC file (14 cross-spectra × 40 bandpowers each — 20 E-mode + 20 B-mode). 20 log-spaced bins from ℓ=40 to ℓ=3000 (decision: `bandpower-binning-20-log-spaced-3628f69e`).

**Covariance.** Block diagonal Knox covariance included. Off-diagonal blocks (cross-bin, cross-method correlations) not computed — this is a limitation for joint inference but sufficient for per-spectrum significance tests. The full joint covariance used by `evaluate_fiducial_likelihood` comes from the PKL files (`build_likelihood_input`), not from SACC.

**Downstream.** SACC files feed into `validate_sacc` (completeness check) and were intended for `eDR1like` likelihood evaluation. In practice, the eDR1like pipeline reads PKL format (see `edr1like-likelihood-reads-pkl-a6201d1d`), so SACC files are a reference format and exchange medium, while PKL provides the actual likelihood input.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/combine_cross_spectra.py`

nodes.title: ACT vs SPT cross-spectra comparison
nodes.body:
Compares the same Euclid shear × CMB κ cross-spectrum measured with two independent CMB experiments: ACT DR6 (baseline, full-sky lensing reconstruction) and SPT-3G (winter field, GMV estimator). Since the Euclid tracer is identical in both, any significant difference reveals CMB-side systematics — foreground residuals, reconstruction biases, or noise mismodeling.

**Test.** For each (method, bin) pair, compute chi2 on the difference C^{ACT}_ℓ - C^{SPT}_ℓ assuming independent experiments (no covariance between ACT and SPT). This is a conservative test — the two experiments share some sky overlap and CMB signal, so they're not perfectly independent. Anti-correlated noise between experiments would make the difference test more sensitive.

**Results.** 14/14 comparisons pass at 5% significance. Median PTE = 0.639 (well-calibrated — not suspiciously high or low). Median chi2/dof = 0.86. The experiments agree across all bins and both shear methods.

**Interpretation.** This is the strongest evidence that neither CMB experiment introduces systematic biases into the cross-correlation. Importantly, this consistency holds even though ACT and SPT show different *amplitudes* relative to theory (ACT Â = 0.93, SPT Â = 0.76 with IA). The ACT-SPT agreement test compares the *shape* of the spectrum, not its absolute amplitude — the normalization difference is absorbed into per-experiment calibration. The SPT normalization deficit (see `spt-normalization-deficit-2cbccf68`) is a uniform suppression across ℓ, consistent with a CMB-side multiplicative bias rather than ℓ-dependent systematics.

**Contamination asymmetry.** Despite the spectra being consistent, the systematic contamination analysis reveals an asymmetry: ACT is contaminated by galactic stellar density and extinction (bias significance up to 8σ in bin 1), while SPT is clean (<0.1σ shifts after template marginalization). This doesn't violate the consistency test because the contamination metric X has the same sign as the signal — it inflates ACT rather than distorting it.

**Evidence.** Per-wildcard JSON at `results/claims/plot_act_vs_spt/`. Aggregated by `aggregate_act_vs_spt_evidence`.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_act_vs_spt.py`

nodes.title: Redshift trend: signal increases with tomographic bin
nodes.body:
All 6 tomographic bins plotted on a single panel per (method, CMB) combination, testing whether the cross-correlation amplitude increases with source redshift as predicted by lensing physics. The CMB κ lensing kernel peaks at z ~ 2 and has increasing overlap with higher-redshift source galaxies, so bins 5 and 6 should have the strongest signal.

**Layout.** Single panel with 6 color-coded curves (HUSL palette). Each bin's ℓ·C_ℓ with Knox error bars. Two theory overlays per bin: vanilla Planck 2018 (solid, faded) and NLA IA (dashed, prominent). The IA suppression is visually clear at low-z bins (1-3) where GI contribution is largest, and converges to vanilla at high-z (bins 5-6) where IA is negligible. Legend shows per-bin S/N and identifies line styles.

**Metric.** SNR monotonicity: how many consecutive bin pairs show increasing S/N. Decision fiber `snr-monotonicity-for-redshift-774268b5` documents why SNR is preferred over mean ℓ·C_ℓ as the amplitude metric — mean ℓ·C_ℓ suffers from cancellation between positive low-ℓ and noisy high-ℓ bins.

**Results.** Signal increases with bin as expected. S/N ranges 5.0-11.6, highest in bin 6. Theory curves track the data ordering. 10 plots produced (2 methods × 5 CMB experiments). 3/10 strictly SNR-monotonic — non-monotonicity at low-z bins where noise dominates.

**What the IA theory overlay reveals.** The vanilla and IA theory curves diverge most at low redshift: bin 1 IA curve is 36% below vanilla (strong GI contamination at z ~ 0.3), while bin 6 curves nearly coincide (4% difference at z ~ 1.5). This makes the redshift trend plot a visual diagnostic for IA — a reader can see that the low-z data points fall systematically between the two theory curves, and that the NLA model's redshift-dependent suppression tracks the data's departure from vanilla theory.

**Connection to contamination.** Bins 1-2 are also where ACT systematic contamination is strongest (bin 1 bias significance = 7.4σ from stellar density). The IA and contamination effects act in opposite directions at low-z: IA suppresses the predicted signal (moving theory down toward data), while contamination boosts the measured signal (moving data up toward theory). After template marginalization, the low-z ACT amplitude drops significantly. The redshift trend plot with raw data thus shows a *coincidentally good* fit to IA theory at low-z for ACT — an effect that is partly contamination masquerading as signal.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_redshift_trend.py`

nodes.title: lensmc vs metacal method comparison
nodes.body:
Compares cross-spectra from two independent shear measurement methods: lensmc (forward-model PSF fitting) and metacal (metacalibration with sheared images). Same tomographic bin × same CMB experiment, differing only in how galaxy shapes were measured. Consistency validates the shear pipeline — if both methods agree, neither introduces a measurement-specific bias into the cross-correlation.

**Layout.** Two-panel figure per comparison. Top: overlaid ℓ·C_ℓ with error bars for both methods. Bottom: fractional difference (lensmc - metacal) / mean, showing where the methods diverge relative to signal amplitude.

**Interpretation.** The RMS fractional difference is a convenient summary statistic but unreliable at low S/N — noise-dominated high-ℓ bins inflate the RMS without implying real method disagreement. Visual agreement across the ℓ-range where error bars are small is the more reliable diagnostic. Chi2 on the difference tests the null hypothesis that both methods measure the same underlying spectrum.

**Correlated-noise subtlety.** The median χ²/dof = 0.14 and PTE = 1.00 across 35 comparisons look suspiciously tight, but this is expected: lensmc and metacal use the *same galaxies* and the *same CMB map*, so their noise is highly correlated. The Knox formula computes variance assuming independent noise realizations — it overestimates the variance of the *difference* between correlated measurements, deflating the chi2. The test confirms consistency but has limited discriminating power. A proper test would require a covariance for the difference spectrum that accounts for the shared noise, which would require either simulations or an analytic model of the noise correlation.

**14 comparisons** (7 bins × 2 CMB, including all+bins 1-6). Methods agree well across all combinations. No evidence of shear measurement bias.

**Contamination sensitivity differs.** Despite the methods producing consistent cross-spectra, they respond differently to systematic contamination. Template marginalization (see `deprojected-likelihood-joint-26b1588e`) shows both methods shift by similar amounts after marginalization (ΔÂ ≈ -0.07 for both). However, naive template subtraction revealed a larger asymmetry — ΔÂ = -0.21 (metacal) vs -0.13 (lensmc) — because metacal's noisier templates injected more noise into the naive chi2. The marginalized results properly handle this, but the underlying contamination coupling is still stronger for metacal: its S/N drops from 19σ to 13σ (31% reduction) vs lensmc's 20σ to 15σ (25% reduction). The different shear weight maps couple differently to spatially-varying systematics. This argues for preferring lensmc for contamination-sensitive analyses, though both methods give consistent final results.

**10 plots for SPT variants** (bins × 5 CMB experiments). The method comparison extends to SPT TT, PP, MV estimators. All show the same pattern: methods agree, no estimator-dependent bias.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_method_comparison.py`

nodes.title: CMB kappa x systematic null tests: galactic extinction contaminates both sides
nodes.body:
The CMB-side leg of the contamination diagnostic: cross-spectra between CMB κ maps and systematic templates. Required to compute the full DES×SPT contamination metric X^f_S(ℓ) = C^{κS}_ℓ · C^{fS}_ℓ / C^{SS}_ℓ.

**What it tests.** Cross-correlations are immune to systematics that contaminate only one tracer — the key advantage over auto-spectra. The contamination metric is non-zero only if a systematic correlates with *both* κ and the Euclid tracer simultaneously. C^{κS} measures whether the systematic leaks into the CMB lensing reconstruction (e.g. dust biasing the CMB gradient estimator).

**Results.** 25 spectra (5 sysmaps × 5 CMB experiments). Galactic extinction strongly contaminates all CMB κ reconstructions: ACT (chi2=68.6/20, PTE=0), SPT GMV (chi2=65.3/20, PTE=0), SPT TT (chi2=48.9/20, PTE=0.0003), SPT PP and MV similar. All other systematics (stellar density, exposures, SAA, zodiacal) are clean across all estimators. This parallels DES×SPT findings (2203.12440) where dust was the dominant contaminant.

Importantly, galactic extinction contamination is **CMB-estimator-independent** — all 4 SPT estimators (GMV, TT, PP, MV) plus ACT show comparable contamination. This confirms the dust signal is in the CMB sky, not an estimator artifact.

**C^{SS} resolved.** The full X metric now uses C^{SS} from systematic auto-spectra (stored as `cl_sys_auto` in NPZ files). See fiber `contamination-metric-needs-c-ss-6e451610`.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/compute_cmbk_systematic_cross_spectrum.py`

nodes.title: Theory comparison: Planck 2018 overlay with chi2 and amplitude diagnostics
nodes.body:
Overlays measured C^{Eκ}_ℓ cross-spectra with Planck 2018 fiducial theory predictions across all 6 tomographic bins. The primary test of whether our measurement is consistent with standard ΛCDM cosmology.

**Layout.** 2×3 grid (one panel per bin). Each panel shows: data with Knox error bars (points), binned theory (thick black line matching data resolution via bandpower windows), unbinned theory (thin gray line showing the continuous prediction). Chi2/dof annotation per panel.

**What to look for.** (1) Shape — theory peaks at ℓ ~ 100-200 and declines; data should follow. (2) Amplitude — data consistent with theory within error bars. (3) Redshift ordering — higher bins have larger amplitude (more lensing kernel overlap). (4) Low-ℓ outliers — expected from cosmic variance at large scales, especially in high-z bins with fewer modes.

**Results.** Data tracks theory in shape across all bins. Now overlays NLA IA prediction (dashed crimson) alongside vanilla (solid black). IA suppression is redshift-dependent: ~36% at bin 1 (low-z), ~4% at bin 6 (high-z) — matching NLA-z physics where GI term peaks at low-to-intermediate z. ACT vanilla chi2=114/120 (PTE=0.63); with IA chi2=106/120 (PTE=0.82) — improved. SPT vanilla chi2=249/120 (PTE≈0); with IA chi2=226/120 (PTE≈0) — improved but still fails. The ACT×IA agreement suggests IA is the dominant missing ingredient for ACT. SPT requires additional calibration correction.

**Assumptions.** Fiducial cosmology documented in fiber `fiducial-cosmology-planck-2018-f1a59f23`. Theory computed via `compute_theory_cls`.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_theory_comparison.py`

nodes.title: Contamination metric: full X = C^{κS}·C^{fS}/C^{SS} negligible for all systematics
nodes.body:
Quantifies how much each systematic contaminates the cross-correlation signal, following the approach of DES×SPT (2203.12440, §A.4). The contamination metric X^f_S(ℓ) = C^{κS}_ℓ · C^{fS}_ℓ / C^{SS}_ℓ has the same units as C^{fκ}_ℓ and measures how much the correlated systematic component biases the cross-spectrum. A systematic must correlate with *both* tracers simultaneously to contaminate — the key insight for cross-correlations.

**Inputs.** Three upstream computations: C^{κS} (from `compute_cmbk_systematic_cross_spectrum`), C^{fS} (from `compute_systematic_cross_spectrum`), and C^{SS} (stored as `cl_sys_auto` in the NPZ files, or using the proxy product C^{κS}·C^{fS} if unavailable).

**Reference band.** The plot includes 1% and 10% of the signal statistical uncertainty σ(C^{fκ}) as shaded reference bands. DES×SPT uses PTE > 0.01 for X consistent with zero as the threshold for concern.

**Results (full X metric).** C^{SS} now stored as `cl_sys_auto` in systematic NPZ files. The full X = C^{κS}·C^{fS}/C^{SS} metric is computed for all 50 combinations (5 sysmaps × 2 methods × 5 CMB experiments, including SPT TT/PP/MV variants). Contamination is sub-percent of signal σ for ALL systematics across ALL CMB estimators. Despite galactic extinction individually contaminating both κ (chi2 ~ 49-69/20) and shear (chi2/dof up to 9), the combined contamination in cross-correlation units is negligible. This confirms the fundamental advantage of cross-correlations over auto-spectra — and the result is CMB-estimator-independent.

**Noise bias caveat.** C^{SS} has noise bias from sparse systematic maps at nside 16384→2048 — noise inflates the denominator, making X a lower bound on the true contamination. Since contamination is negligible even as a lower bound, the conclusion is robust.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_contamination_metric.py`

nodes.title: Individual cross-spectrum plots: 28 positive spectra visualized
nodes.body:
Visual inspection of every individual cross-spectrum. The first-order check before any comparison or combination: does each spectrum look physical?

**Layout.** Two-panel figure per spectrum. Top: E-mode (signal) with Knox error bars, ℓ·C_ℓ scaling. Bottom: B-mode (null check) — should be consistent with zero for a real lensing signal. Annotation box shows SNR and PTE.

**B-mode caveat.** At the partial sky coverage of individual tomographic bins (fsky ~ 0.01), NaMaster E→B leakage can produce B-mode amplitudes up to ~93% of E-mode. The B-mode variance is ~500× smaller than E-mode (since C_BB << C_EE), so elevated B-mode chi2 is expected from leakage, not from systematics. B-mode is diagnostic, not a reliable null test at this fsky.

**28 spectra.** All show positive E-mode signal. S/N ranges from ~3 (bin1, small lensing kernel overlap) to ~12 (bin6, maximum overlap). The visual impression is the starting point for every downstream comparison — method differences, CMB experiment consistency, and redshift trends all build on these individual measurements.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_cross_spectrum.py`

nodes.title: Systematic null test heatmap: Knox chi2/dof and PTE summary
nodes.body:
Summary visualization of all 70 systematic null tests in a single figure — the at-a-glance diagnostic for whether observational systematics contaminate the shear signal.

**Layout.** Two-row figure. Top: chi2/dof heatmap with coolwarm colormap centered on 1 (blue < 1, red > 1). Bottom: PTE heatmap with RdYlGn colormap (red = low PTE = contaminated, green = high PTE = clean). Columns = methods, rows within each panel = systematic maps, sub-columns = bins.

**Covariance choice.** Knox formula is primary, not NaMaster Gaussian. At the small fsky (~0.01) of individual tomographic bins, NaMaster overestimates variance by 10-130×, washing out real contamination. Knox gives properly calibrated chi2/dof ≈ 1 under H0 (documented in fiber `suspiciously-good-ptes-in-dbf44cbb` and `systematic-pte-overestimation-89f57308`).

**Results.** Four systematics (stellar density, exposures, SAA, zodiacal) are clean: chi2/dof ~ 1, PTE well-distributed. Galactic extinction is the exception: chi2/dof 1.3-9.0, PTE ≈ 0 for most bins, strongest in high-z bins (5, 6). This is consistent with Galactic dust affecting the shear measurement through PSF modeling residuals or photometric calibration.

**What this misses.** The shear × systematic null test (C^{fS}) tests only whether the systematic correlates with the Euclid tracer. A systematic that correlates with CMB κ but not shear (or vice versa) won't contaminate the cross-correlation. The full contamination diagnostic requires the DES×SPT-style metric X = C^{κS}·C^{fS}/C^{SS}, computed in `plot_contamination_metric`. Critically, the contamination metric X is *negligible* for all systematics — even galactic extinction — because the correlated component between both tracers simultaneously is much smaller than the individual correlations. This is the fundamental advantage of cross-correlations over auto-spectra.

**The stellar density surprise.** Stellar density shows only marginal failure in this null test (5/14 fail), suggesting it's a minor contaminant. But the bias budget analysis (`plot_systematic_budget`) reveals it's actually the *dominant* ACT contaminant — worse than galactic extinction — because its correlation with CMB κ is stronger. The null test alone gives a misleading picture; the full contamination metric is needed.

**Evidence.** Aggregated at `results/claims/plot_systematic_null_tests/evidence.json` with per-systematic n_fail, median chi2/dof, median PTE.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_systematic_null_tests.py`

nodes.title: SACC file validation: tracers, n(z), covariance complete
nodes.body:
Automated validation of the combined SACC file, run after `combine_shear_cross_spectra` packages 28 individual NPZ files into a single data product. Catches packaging errors before the SACC file is used downstream — missing tracers, unnormalized n(z), dimension mismatches between data vector and covariance.

**What it checks.** (1) All 15 tracers defined: 14 NZTracer (7 bins × 2 methods, each with z and n(z) arrays) + 1 MiscTracer (CMB κ). (2) n(z) physically sensible: non-negative, integrates to ~1 for each bin. (3) Data vector complete: 560 data points (14 spectra × 40 bandpowers — 20 E-mode + 20 B-mode). (4) Covariance matrix shape (560 × 560) matches data vector. (5) Covariance positive semi-definite (eigenvalue check). Run independently per CMB experiment (ACT, SPT).

**Why this matters.** Dimension mismatches between the data vector and covariance cause silent errors in likelihood evaluation — wrong chi2 values with no error message. The tracer check ensures n(z) curves are properly associated with each cross-spectrum, which is critical for theory prediction. A missing or swapped n(z) would bias the theory C_ℓ comparison.

**Evidence.** Validation results stored at `results/claims/validate_sacc/evidence.json` with pass/fail per check.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/validate_sacc.py`

nodes.title: Catalog validation summary: compact heatmap of 28 comparisons
nodes.body:
Compact summary of all 28 catalog vs map comparisons in a single heatmap figure, complementing the per-wildcard overlay plots from `plot_catalog_vs_map_comparison`.

**Layout.** Two-panel heatmap. Left: Pearson correlation between catalog-based and map-based C_ℓ (should be > 0.99). Right: RMS fractional difference (should be < 5%). Rows = bins, columns = method × CMB experiment.

**What it validates.** If catalog-based spectra (NmtFieldCatalog, bypassing pixelization) agree with map-based spectra (NmtField, standard pipeline), then map construction introduces no significant bias. The two approaches use the same galaxies and same CMB κ map but different computational paths — agreement is a strong pipeline consistency check.

**Results.** 28/28 comparisons show correlation 0.986-1.000, median RMS fractional difference well below 5%. Map-based pipeline validated. No systematic trend with bin number — the lowest correlations (~0.986) occur in the lowest-S/N bins where noise dominates, not from a systematic map-making bias.

**RMS weighting.** The RMS fractional difference uses S/N weighting (iteration 7): each bandpower weighted by its inverse Knox variance. This downweights noise-dominated high-ℓ bins that inflate the unweighted RMS to 12-1380% without implying real disagreement. Weighted range: 3-46%.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_catalog_validation_summary.py`

nodes.title: Theory C_ℓ computation: eDR1like with Planck 2018 fiducial
nodes.body:
Generates fiducial theory C^{Eκ}_ℓ predictions for each tomographic bin using `eDR1like.Theory` backed by CCL (Core Cosmology Library) with CAMB as the Boltzmann solver.

**Cosmology.** Planck 2018 best-fit (TT,TE,EE+lowE+lensing): A_s=2.105×10^{-9}, n_s=0.9665, H_0=67.66, Ω_b=0.04897, Ω_c=0.2607. Galaxy bias b=1 (constant), no magnification.

**Two theory models computed per bin:**

1. **Vanilla** (lensing only) — galaxy-galaxy lensing with no intrinsic alignment. This provides the baseline for comparing data amplitude and shape. When A_hat < 1, the deficit flags missing physics (IA, calibration) or CMB-side normalization issues.

2. **NLA intrinsic alignment** — nonlinear alignment model with A_IA=1.72, eta_IA=-0.41 (eDR1like defaults, NLA-z parameterization). CCL handles the physics internally: `WeakLensingTracer` with `use_A_ia=True` computes C_1 * rho_cr * Omega_m / D(z) * A_IA * (1+z)^eta_IA. IA suppresses the cross-spectrum at low redshift (36% in bin 1, 4% in bin 6), resolving the ACT amplitude deficit from 3σ to 1.3σ. See fiber `nla-intrinsic-alignment-cb1dea0b`.

**Binning.** Unbinned theory C_ℓ computed at every multipole, then binned through NaMaster's bandpower window functions (Bbl) to match the data resolution. This ensures fair comparison — binned theory accounts for the mode-coupling matrix and bandpower averaging. Bbl was already stored in the cross-spectrum NPZ files (`bpws` key, shape (2,20,2,3001)); E-mode extraction: `bpws[0,:,0,:]`.

**n(z).** Source redshift distributions from `RR2_v2_Nz_WL_C2020_sel_pv.fits` (6 bins, 3000 z-points on linspace(0,6), sum-normalized). Method-specific (lensmc vs metacal have different selection functions).

**Output.** NPZ with keys: `cl_theory_bin{1-6}` (vanilla unbinned), `cl_binned_bin{1-6}` (vanilla binned), `cl_ia_theory_bin{1-6}` (IA unbinned), `cl_ia_binned_bin{1-6}` (IA binned), plus `ell_theory` and metadata. Downstream consumers: theory comparison, residual analysis, likelihood evaluation, signal overview, deprojected likelihood.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/compute_theory_cls.py`

nodes.title: Catalog vs map comparison: 28 overlay plots
nodes.body:
Per-wildcard overlay of catalog-based and map-based cross-spectra for visual inspection. The complement to the summary heatmap in `plot_catalog_validation_summary` — here you see the full ℓ-dependence, not just summary statistics.

**Why this matters.** The map-based pipeline converts catalogs to pixelized maps (nside 2048) before computing spectra via NaMaster. This introduces potential biases: sub-pixel averaging, weight map artifacts, and pixelization window function effects. The catalog-based pipeline (`NmtFieldCatalog`) bypasses all of this — it works directly from galaxy positions and ellipticities. Agreement between the two confirms that map-making is not introducing artifacts. This is especially important for the low-number-density high-z bins where pixel occupancy is sparse.

**Layout.** Single panel per comparison. Both spectra overlaid with shared x-axis (ℓ) and ℓ·C_ℓ scaling. Error bars from map-based Knox covariance (no covariance available for catalog-based, since NaMaster doesn't support covariance for NmtFieldCatalog). The visual comparison is the primary diagnostic — differences should be invisible compared to error bars.

**28 comparisons.** Differences are sub-percent in well-measured bandpowers. Any visible discrepancy at high ℓ is consistent with noise — catalog-based and map-based spectra use different internal representations of the same underlying field. S/N-weighted RMS fractional differences range 3-46% (SNR weighting from `plot_catalog_validation_summary` downweights noise-dominated high-ℓ bins).

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_catalog_vs_map_comparison.py`

nodes.title: SPT estimator comparison: GMV vs TT vs PP vs MV foreground sensitivity
nodes.body:
## What this tests

SPT-3G winter data provides CMB lensing maps from four estimator variants with genuinely different foreground sensitivity:

- **GMV** (global minimum variance): Baseline. Uses all T+E+B quadratic pairs. Highest SNR but most foreground-exposed.
- **TT** (temperature-only): Most sensitive to tSZ contamination from galaxy clusters. If TT cross-spectra differ from GMV, tSZ leakage is biasing the lensing reconstruction.
- **PP** (polarization-only): Cleanest for foregrounds (no tSZ in polarization), but noisiest. The "gold standard" for foreground-free lensing.
- **MV** (SQE minimum variance): Similar to GMV but from the separable quadratic estimator pipeline. Tests pipeline-level consistency.

All variants share the same mask (same survey footprint). Differences in cross-spectra isolate the effect of foreground contamination in the CMB lensing reconstruction.

## Methodology

Cross-correlate each SPT estimator variant with the same Euclid shear maps (2 methods x 7 bins = 14 combinations per variant, 42 new cross-spectra total). Compare estimator variants by:

1. **Overlay plot**: All four variants on one panel per method+bin, with Knox error bars.
2. **Residual panel**: Pull = (variant - GMV) / sigma_diff, where sigma_diff combines independent errors in quadrature.
3. **Chi2 consistency test**: chi2 for each variant vs GMV, with PTE > 0.01 as the threshold for concern.

## Assumptions

- SPT estimator variants use the same mask: `mask2048_border_apod_mask_threshold0.1_allghz_dense.fits`.
- Knox covariance treats experiments as independent (no shared noise between estimators — valid since different quadratic pairs).
- Cross-spectrum errors are dominated by the Euclid shape noise × CMB noise, so different estimator noise levels primarily affect error bar size, not the signal.

## Evidence

- Rule: `plot_spt_estimator_comparison` in `workflow/rules/explorations.smk`
- Outputs: `results/claims/plot_spt_estimator_comparison/spt_estimator_{method}_bin{bin}.png`
- Evidence: `results/claims/plot_spt_estimator_comparison/evidence.json`
- Addresses: validation test proposal #3 (cross-correlation with CMB lensing maps from different estimators)

## Connection to DES x SPT

The DES x SPT analysis (2203.12440) checks estimator consistency as part of their foreground systematic tests. They find TT and MV are consistent for galaxy density x kappa. We extend this to shear x kappa with Euclid data.

nodes.title: Jackknife null test: random sign flip validates galaxy-side systematics
nodes.body:
Tests for galaxy-side systematics by destroying the coherent cosmological signal in the shear maps while preserving noise properties. Each galaxy's ellipticity is multiplied by a random sign (±1, seed=42), producing null shear maps that have zero cosmological signal in expectation but identical noise structure, pixel coverage, and weight maps. Cross-correlating these null maps with CMB κ should yield spectra consistent with zero — any excess signals systematic contamination on the galaxy side (PSF residuals, selection effects, calibration errors correlated with sky position).

**Pipeline.** Three rules chained: `build_jackknife_null_maps` constructs the randomized shear maps from the catalog (SLURM, 16GB); `compute_jackknife_null_spectrum` cross-correlates with CMB κ using the standard `compute_cross_spectrum.py` script unmodified (same workspace cache — identical masks means identical coupling matrices); `plot_jackknife_null_test` produces summary panels with Knox error bars and chi2/PTE statistics.

**Coverage.** 24 null spectra: 2 methods (lensmc, metacal) × 6 tomographic bins × 2 CMB experiments (ACT, SPT-GMV). Each spectrum has 20 bandpowers.

**Results.** All 24 null spectra consistent with zero. Median Knox chi2/dof = 0.88, median PTE = 0.617, 0 failures at 5% threshold. This rules out galaxy-side systematics that survive random sign flipping — the dominant class of additive systematics (PSF leakage, detection bias). It does not test multiplicative biases (shear calibration), which are invariant to sign flips.

**Limitation.** A single random realization (seed=42). Multiple realizations would provide better statistics on the null distribution, but the current test is sufficient to detect large additive systematics. The random sign flip also does not test for systematics that cancel between E and B modes.

**Scripts**: `workflow/scripts/build_jackknife_null_maps.py`, `workflow/scripts/compute_cross_spectrum.py` (reused), `workflow/scripts/plot_jackknife_null_test.py`

nodes.title: Theory residual analysis: amplitude fits disentangle calibration from shape
nodes.body:
Fits a single amplitude parameter A_hat to each bin × CMB experiment cross-spectrum, then tests whether the residual shape (after removing the amplitude degree of freedom) is consistent with the theory curve.

## Method

For each tomographic bin b and CMB experiment c, the best-fit amplitude is:

  A_hat = Σ(d_ℓ · t_ℓ / σ²_ℓ) / Σ(t²_ℓ / σ²_ℓ)

where d_ℓ is measured C^{Eκ}, t_ℓ is binned Planck 2018 theory, and σ²_ℓ is Knox variance. The shape chi2 uses dof = N_bandpowers - 1 (one fitted parameter). This separates two failure modes: multiplicative offset (A ≠ 1, good shape) vs genuine shape discrepancy (poor shape chi2 regardless of A).

## Results

### Vanilla theory (lensing only)

| CMB | Method | mean A | σ from 1 | shape PTE |
|-----|--------|--------|----------|-----------|
| ACT | lensmc | 0.844 ± 0.050 | 3.1σ | 0.83 |
| ACT | metacal | 0.815 ± 0.052 | 3.5σ | 0.73 |
| SPT-GMV | lensmc | 0.694 ± 0.045 | 6.8σ | ~0 |
| SPT-GMV | metacal | 0.697 ± 0.048 | 6.3σ | ~0 |

### With NLA IA (A_IA=1.72, eta=-0.41)

| CMB | Method | mean A (IA) | σ from 1 | Improvement |
|-----|--------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| ACT | lensmc | 0.931 ± 0.055 | 1.3σ | **resolved** |
| ACT | metacal | 0.893 ± 0.057 | 1.9σ | near unity |
| SPT-GMV | lensmc | 0.762 ± 0.049 | 4.9σ | partial |
| SPT-GMV | metacal | 0.758 ± 0.053 | 4.6σ | partial |

## Interpretation

**IA resolves ACT deficit.** The NLA model with eDR1like default parameters (A_IA=1.72, eta=-0.41) brings ACT to A_hat=0.93 (1.3σ from unity). The IA suppression is redshift-dependent (36% at bin 1, 4% at bin 6) — matching NLA-z physics where GI term peaks at low-z where source-lens alignment is largest. The remaining ~7% may be multiplicative shear calibration (m ~ -0.03) or baryonic feedback.

**SPT deficit persists (~24% after IA).** IA explains ~10% of the ~30% SPT deficit, leaving A=0.76 (4.9σ from 1). This additional deficit is method-independent (lensmc ≈ metacal) and CMB-side: possible SPT lensing response normalization, mean-field subtraction, or N0/N1 bias. Not addressable from the Euclid side.

**Two cleanly separated effects.** (1) Common suppression (~10-15%) explained by NLA IA — expected physics, not a systematic. (2) SPT-specific normalization (~20-24%) — requires investigation by SPT team or alternative normalization.

nodes.title: 31 tapestry nodes
nodes.body:
Synthesis of the full Euclid × CMB-κ cross-correlation analysis into a single 4-panel figure. This is the leaf node of the tapestry DAG — it reads aggregate evidence from all upstream tapestry nodes and distills the key numbers.

## What it shows

**(a) Detection significance.** Knox S/N per tomographic bin for lensmc × ACT and lensmc × SPT-GMV. All 12 baseline cross-spectra detected at >3σ (Knox), with 11/12 at >5σ. SNR rises monotonically with bin number — consistent with increasing lensing kernel overlap at higher source redshift. ACT and SPT have comparable statistical power (median ~7–8σ per bin). Two headline numbers: **raw combined S/N** (ACT 20σ, SPT 21σ — diagonal Knox sum over bins) and **marginalized S/N** (ACT 15σ, SPT 14σ — after joint Fisher fit accounting for systematic template contamination). The marginalized number is the definitive detection significance; the raw number is the data-level quantity before systematic handling.

**(b) Consistency tests.** Four independent cross-checks, all passing: ACT vs SPT (14/14 at 5%, median PTE 0.64), lensmc vs metacal (35/35, PTE 1.0), SPT estimator variants (14/14, PTE 1.0), catalog vs map pipeline (28/28, median correlation 0.998). The analysis is robust to every methodological and instrumental variation tested.

**(c) Null tests.** Eight independent null/validation tests, all shown as horizontal bars of median chi2/dof with Knox covariance. Five systematic maps (14 bins each = 70 total): galactic extinction fails 10/14, stellar density marginal (5/14), others clean. Three additional validation tests: jackknife sign-flip null (0/24 fail, median chi2/dof = 0.88), simulation null using 11 SPT sim kappa maps (1/66 false alarm, chi2/dof = 0.92), and spatial variation north-vs-south patch split (0/6 fail at ell>100, chi2/dof = 1.34). The contamination metric (X^f_S, DES×SPT Eq. A.4.1) shows |X| ~ 10^{-9} for all systematics — negligible vs signal ~10^{-7}.

**(d) Theory amplitude.** Best-fit amplitude A_hat per bin, shown for both vanilla Planck 2018 theory and NLA intrinsic alignment model (A_IA=1.72, eta_IA=-0.41). Vanilla: ACT 0.84±0.05 (3.1σ low), SPT 0.69±0.05 (6.8σ low). With NLA IA: ACT 0.93±0.05 (1.3σ — resolved), SPT 0.76±0.05 (4.9σ — persists). The plot shows vanilla as faded markers and IA as prominent markers, making the correction visible per bin. IA suppression is strongest at low-z (36% in bin 1, 4% in bin 6), matching NLA-z physics. Two effects cleanly separated: common IA suppression (~10%) + SPT-specific normalization deficit (~24%). The SPT deficit is CMB-side — not addressable from Euclid data. See `spt-normalization-deficit-2cbccf68`.

**(d) annotations.** Two insets on panel (d) tell the contamination story:

- *Right inset* — joint likelihood chi2/dof from `evaluate_fiducial_likelihood`: ACT IA chi2/dof = 0.88 (PTE 0.82) — excellent fit; SPT IA chi2/dof = 1.88 (PTE 0.00) — shape mismatch persists.
- *Left inset* — after template marginalization from `evaluate_deprojected_likelihood`: ACT Â(IA) = 0.86 (Δ = -0.07 from original 0.93), profile chi2/dof = 0.75 (PTE 0.98); SPT Â = 0.75 (Δ = -0.01), chi2/dof = 1.61 (PTE 0.00). The marginalization jointly fits signal amplitude and systematic contamination amplitudes without noise injection (see `deprojected-likelihood-joint-26b1588e` for the full S/N paradox resolution). ACT signal is consistent with Planck 2018 + NLA IA after proper contamination handling. SPT deficit persists — confirmed as CMB-side normalization, not systematic contamination.

## Technical notes

- Uses `ancient()` for evidence inputs to avoid DAG cascade through upstream computation rules
- Falls back to NPZ Knox SNR computation when evidence files are corrupted (2 files were overwritten by comparison rule collision)
- Reads sim null and spatial variation evidence via individual_evidence artifact paths in aggregate JSON
- Reads fiducial likelihood chi2 for panel (d) annotation
- Rule: `plot_analysis_summary` in `workflow/rules/explorations.smk` (localrule)
- Script: `workflow/scripts/plot_analysis_summary.py`

nodes.title: Likelihood input builder: NPZ→PKL bridge to eDR1like
nodes.body:
Bridges the cross-correlation pipeline outputs to the eDR1like Cobaya-compatible likelihood. The pipeline stores spectra in NPZ format with NaMaster conventions; the likelihood reads NumPy pickle (PKL) files with a specific dictionary structure.

## What it does

Reads per-bin NPZ files for one method × one CMB experiment and assembles a PKL dictionary:

- **`cls`**: Dict of 6 spectra (`kappa_g0` through `kappa_g5`). Each has `cl` (E-mode bandpower), `Bbl` (bandpower window matrix, 20×3001), `leff` (effective multipoles), and `nl` (noise placeholder).
- **`cov`**: Block-diagonal 6×6 list-of-lists with Knox variance on diagonal blocks. No cross-bin correlations.
- **`cov_all`**: Flattened 120×120 covariance matrix.
- **`dndz`**: Per-bin redshift distributions from `RR2_v2_Nz_WL_C2020_sel_pv.fits`, normalized to unit integral.

## Key extraction

The critical step is extracting `Bbl = bpws[0, :, 0, :]` from the 4D NaMaster bandpower windows. This selects the E-mode × scalar coupling (first spin component), yielding a `(n_ell, lmax+1)` matrix that bins theory C_ℓ into measured bandpowers via `theory_binned = Bbl @ cl_theory`.

## Naming convention

Our 1-indexed Euclid bins map to 0-indexed likelihood bins: bin1→g0, bin2→g1, ..., bin6→g5. Spectrum names: `kappa_g{i}` for CMB κ × shear bin i.

## Limitations

- Covariance is Knox diagonal only — sufficient for detection significance but not joint parameter inference. Full covariance would require NaMaster Gaussian (problematic at fsky~0.01) or simulations.
- No CMB κ transfer function applied — likelihood can handle this via `do_kappa_tf=True` if transfer function data is provided.
- Only κ×γ spectra — no shear auto-spectra (γ×γ). The full 3×2pt PKL would need additional inputs.

## Script

`workflow/scripts/build_likelihood_input.py` — snakemake rule `build_likelihood_input` in `explorations.smk`.

nodes.title: Fiducial likelihood evaluation: joint chi2 and detection significance
nodes.body:
Evaluates the full 6-bin data vector against Planck 2018 fiducial theory, using the PKL likelihood inputs and theory C_ℓ. This is the definitive end-to-end validation of the likelihood infrastructure — PKL assembly, bandpower window application, covariance inversion, and chi2 computation.

## What it computes

For each method × CMB experiment:

1. **Detection significance**: S/N = sqrt(d^T C^{-1} d), data vector against zero. Validates the combined Knox quadrature calculation.
2. **Fiducial goodness-of-fit**: chi2/dof and PTE for data vs Planck 2018 theory (both vanilla and NLA IA). Tests whether the fiducial cosmology is an acceptable fit.
3. **Joint amplitude fit**: A_hat = (d^T C^{-1} t) / (t^T C^{-1} t), the linear amplitude that best scales theory to match data across all 6 bins simultaneously. Shape chi2 (after amplitude fit) tests whether residual deviations are consistent with noise.

## Key results

| Combination | Det. S/N | IA chi2/dof | IA PTE | A_hat(IA) |
|-------------|----------|-------------|--------|-----------|
| lensmc × ACT | 19.8 | 0.88 | 0.82 | 0.93 ± 0.06 |
| metacal × ACT | 18.8 | 0.88 | 0.81 | 0.91 ± 0.06 |
| lensmc × SPT | 21.0 | 1.88 | ≈0 | 0.76 ± 0.05 |
| metacal × SPT | 19.6 | 1.84 | ≈0 | 0.76 ± 0.05 |

## Interpretation

ACT × Euclid is beautifully consistent with Planck 2018 + NLA IA. The NLA model brings A_hat from 0.84 → 0.93 (1.3σ from unity). Shape is excellent (PTE 0.83 after amplitude fit).

SPT × Euclid detects the cross-correlation at >20σ, but Planck 2018 theory is a poor fit at any amplitude. Shape chi2/dof ≈ 1.5 persists after amplitude fit, ruling out a simple normalization offset. This is consistent with the per-bin analysis (fiber `spt-normalization-deficit-2cbccf68`) and method-independent — the deficit is on the CMB side.

## Assumptions

- Block-diagonal Knox covariance (no cross-bin terms). This underestimates the true covariance if tomographic bins overlap in redshift. For ACT the PTE is 0.82, leaving room for this effect. For SPT, even a 50% increase in variance wouldn't bring chi2/dof below 1.2.
- Theory at Planck 2018 best-fit cosmology with NLA IA (A_IA=1.72, eta=-0.41).
- No CMB kappa transfer function correction applied.

nodes.title: Simulation null test: sim kappa x Euclid shear
nodes.body:
Cross-correlate SPT simulation kappa maps with real Euclid shear to validate the absence of spurious correlations and calibrate the Knox covariance.

## Rationale

The jackknife null test (fiber `jackknife-null-test-random-sign-e86273e8`) randomizes the galaxy side while keeping the CMB side fixed. This test does the complement: keeps the galaxies fixed and swaps the CMB kappa map for simulated realizations that share the same noise properties and reconstruction pipeline but have no correlated cosmological signal with the real galaxy field.

SPT provides 11 simulated kappa maps processed through the same `healqest` lensing reconstruction pipeline:
- **Seed 1**: Gaussian CMB realization + instrumental noise
- **Seeds 5001-5010**: Agora simulations (realistic CMB + foregrounds + noise)

None share the real lensing convergence field, so cross-correlation with Euclid shear should be zero.

## What it tests

1. **Spurious correlations**: Any non-zero mean across simulations would indicate pipeline artifacts — e.g., a mean-field bias in the kappa maps that correlates with the galaxy field through shared survey geometry.

2. **Knox covariance calibration**: The bandpower scatter across 11 simulations gives an empirical estimate of the diagonal covariance. Comparing to the Knox prediction (which uses the auto-spectra) validates our error bars. A ratio > 1 means Knox underestimates the true variance.

3. **Foreground contamination**: The Agora sims include realistic foregrounds (tSZ, CIB, radio sources). If foreground residuals in the lensing reconstruction correlate with galaxy positions (through the CIB-LSS connection), the Agora sims would show elevated cross-spectra compared to the Gaussian sim.

## Interpreting the evidence

The `mean_null_snr` values (3.7-5.0) may look alarming for "null" spectra, but are expected. This is the S/N of the *mean* of 11 null spectra, computed as quadrature sum across 20 bandpowers. Under the null, each bandpower contributes ~1 in quadrature, giving expected mean_null_snr ≈ sqrt(20) ≈ 4.5. The observed values are completely consistent.

The empirical variance ratio (0.83 median) means Knox overestimates variance by ~17%. This is expected: Knox uses the total auto-spectra (signal + noise) in the numerator, which is conservative. The effect is larger for high-S/N bins where the signal auto-spectrum contributes more.

## Assumptions

- SPT sim kappa maps have the same masking, resolution, and noise properties as the data map.
- Workspace caching applies: same Euclid shear masks and SPT mask → same coupling matrix as the production cross-spectra. No additional workspace computation needed.
- 11 simulations is marginal for covariance estimation (variance of the variance is ~45% with N=11). The variance ratio diagnostic is indicative, not definitive.

nodes.title: Spatial variation test: north vs south patch cross-spectra consistency
nodes.body:
Split the RR2 footprint into two patches (north and south) and compute independent cross-spectra in each. Tests for spatial systematics on both galaxy and CMB sides — the one dimension the jackknife null test (which randomizes galaxy signs uniformly) does not probe.

**Patches.** Defined by RA/Dec box cuts from visual inspection of the weight map coverage:
- North: RA [63.8°, 77.2°], Dec [−37°, −26.5°]
- South: RA [32°, 70°], Dec [−63.5°, −44°]

Both patches are covered by ACT and SPT. The north patch is smaller in solid angle; the south patch extends to lower declinations where ACT sensitivity degrades.

**Method.** Multiply the shear weight map and CMB mask by a binary patch mask before computing cross-spectra. NaMaster computes a new coupling matrix for each patch geometry — workspaces are not reused from the full-footprint analysis. Knox covariance is computed independently per patch.

**Consistency test.** For each bin, compute the difference Δ = C^{Eκ}_N − C^{Eκ}_S and test against zero using Var(Δ) = Var_N + Var_S (independent patches). Chi2/dof ≈ 1 and PTE well-distributed means no spatial variation detected.

**Scope.** Initial run: lensmc × baseline CMB (ACT + SPT GMV) × 6 bins × 2 patches = 24 cross-spectra. Expandable to metacal and estimator variants if needed.

**Relevance to SPT normalization.** If the SPT normalization deficit (A_hat ≈ 0.76) is spatial, one patch should show a larger deficit than the other. If it's uniform, both patches show the same deficit — pointing to a global calibration issue.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_spatial_variation.py`
**Config**: `spatial_patches` in `config/config.yaml`

nodes.title: Signal overview: ACT + SPT + theory hero figure
nodes.body:
The hero figure for the cross-correlation analysis. Each of 6 panels (2x3 grid, one per tomographic bin) shows measured C_ℓ^{κE} for both ACT DR6 and SPT-3G GMV, overlaid with vanilla Planck 2018 and NLA intrinsic alignment theory predictions. Knox error bars from diagonal covariance.

## What it shows

- **Detection in all bins**: S/N ranges from 5.0σ (lensmc bin 2 × ACT) to 11.7σ (lensmc bin 6 × SPT). All 24 method×bin×CMB combinations detected at >3σ.
- **ACT-SPT agreement in shape**: Both experiments trace the same ℓ-dependence per bin, with slight amplitude offset (SPT systematically lower).
- **SPT normalization deficit**: SPT data sits below theory in most bins, consistent with the ~24% normalization deficit documented in `spt-theory-comparison-fails-b14b26a4`.
- **IA correction magnitude**: The gap between vanilla (black) and NLA IA (crimson dashed) theory is largest at low redshift (bin 1: ~36% suppression) and negligible at high redshift (bin 6: ~4%). This is the NLA-z physics: IA amplitude scales as (1+z)^η with η < 0.

## Visual design

- ACT: salmon circles (#E8788A), SPT: teal squares (#5B9EA6)
- Horizontal offset (×0.97/×1.03) avoids data point overlap
- Unbinned theory as thin background line, binned theory as thick foreground
- Panel titles show per-CMB Knox S/N for quick reference
- log-ℓ x-axis, ℓ·C_ℓ scaling, range ℓ ∈ [30, 4000]

## Quantitative evidence

Chi2/dof vs NLA IA theory per bin×CMB, computed from binned residuals with Knox errors. ACT consistent in all 6 bins (PTE > 0.2). SPT fails in bins 2, 3, and 6 — driven by the normalization deficit that IA alone cannot resolve.

Rule: `plot_signal_overview` in `workflow/rules/explorations.smk` (localrule). Script: `workflow/scripts/plot_signal_overview.py`. One PNG per method (lensmc, metacal).

nodes.title: Systematic bias budget: per-bin contamination significance
nodes.body:
Quantitative summary of systematic contamination using the DES×SPT bias significance metric. The contamination metric X(ℓ) = C^{κS}_ℓ · C^{fS}_ℓ / C^{SS}_ℓ has the same units as the signal C^{Eκ}_ℓ. The bias significance S_bias = sqrt(Σ_ℓ X²/σ²) measures how many σ of bias each systematic contributes, integrating over all ℓ-bins.

**Key finding**: stellar density is the dominant systematic for ACT (4.4σ in bin 5, worse than galactic extinction at 3.7σ). This wasn't apparent from the C^{γS} null tests alone, which flagged galactic extinction as worse — the bias significance weights by the systematic's correlation with *both* tracers simultaneously (C^{κS}/C^{SS}), and stellar density has stronger proportional correlation with CMB κ.

**ACT vs SPT asymmetry**: ACT DR6 has much larger sky coverage than SPT-3G winter field, including regions closer to the galactic plane. This admits more galactic contamination into the cross-correlation footprint. The asymmetry is consistent across all systematics — not specific to one tracer.

**Zodiacal light**: C^{SS} ≈ 0 (auto-power ~10^{-39}), making X undefined. The systematic has no measurable power on the sky, so cannot contaminate by construction. Plotted as dashes.

**Implications**: For ACT, stellar density and galactic extinction contribute multi-σ biases. Whether these biases are large enough to affect parameter constraints depends on the ratio X/C_signal (fractional bias), not just X/σ. The max X/σ ~ 4.4 vs signal S/N ~ 20 suggests a ~20% fractional bias in the worst bin — significant for precision cosmology.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/plot_systematic_budget.py`

nodes.title: Template deprojection: contamination boosts ACT signal
nodes.body:
## What this tests

Template deprojection is the simplest systematic mitigation: subtract the estimated contamination directly from the measured cross-spectrum. Following DES x SPT (2203.12440 section A.4), the expected bias to C^{Ekappa} from systematic S is:

X^f_S(ell) = C^{kappa S}_ell * C^{fS}_ell / C^{SS}_ell

The total contamination is X_total = sum over all 5 systematics (stellar density, galactic extinction, exposures, zodiacal light, SAA). The deprojected spectrum is C^{Ekappa,clean} = C^{Ekappa} - X_total.

## What we found

### ACT contamination is positive (same sign as signal)

The contamination metric X_total is predominantly positive — it adds to the cross-spectrum signal rather than corrupting it with noise. Subtracting it *reduces* the measured amplitude.

### Per-bin impact (ACT lensmc)

| Bin | S_bias (sigma) | A_hat before | A_hat after | Dominant systematic |
|-----|---------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1   | 7.4           | 1.07        | 0.26       | Stellar density   |
| 2   | 3.4           | 0.91        | 0.96       | Galactic extinction |
| 3   | 4.3           | 0.79        | 0.80       | Stellar density   |
| 4   | 5.6           | 1.18        | 1.04       | Stellar density   |
| 5   | 11.0          | 1.03        | 0.77       | Stellar density   |
| 6   | 3.5           | 0.84        | 0.77       | Stellar density   |

Weighted mean A_hat(IA): 0.93 -> 0.80 (delta = -0.13, 2.5 sigma shift).

### SPT is unaffected

SPT weighted mean A_hat(IA): 0.76 -> 0.75 (delta = -0.01, <0.1 sigma). The SPT normalization deficit is NOT from systematic contamination — it is intrinsic (CMB-side normalization or physics).

### Bin 1 is unreliable for ACT

After deprojection, bin 1 retains only 26% of the expected signal. The systematic contamination carried ~70% of the measured cross-spectrum. This bin should be excluded from ACT parameter inference or treated with explicit marginalization.

### Bin 5 shows moderate contamination

A_hat drops from 1.03 to 0.77 — a 25% reduction. S_bias_total = 11.0 sigma (highest of all bins), driven by stellar density at 4.4 sigma.

## Caveats

1. **Lower bound on contamination**: C^{SS} in the denominator has noise bias from pixelization and sparse sampling of systematic maps. Noise inflates the denominator, making X smaller than the true contamination. Noise debiasing would make the deprojection correction LARGER.

2. **Naive subtraction superseded by marginalization**: The per-bin bar chart shows naive template subtraction (Â = 0.80 for ACT lensmc), which **injects template noise** into the chi2 and gives pessimistic amplitudes. The authoritative values from Fisher matrix template marginalization are annotated on the plot: ACT lensmc Â = 0.86±0.06 (profile χ²/dof = 0.75, PTE = 0.98). See fiber `deprojected-likelihood-joint-26b1588e` for the full comparison. The naive per-bin view is diagnostic (shows *where* contamination acts); the joint marginal annotation is the quotable number.

3. **Cross-terms not included**: Template deprojection assumes systematics are independent. Correlated systematic effects would require joint modeling.

## Implications

1. ACT parameter inference should either exclude bins 1 and 5, or marginalize over contamination templates
2. SPT is the cleaner dataset for precision cosmology at low redshift
3. The ACT 20 sigma combined detection is ~14% inflated by stellar density contamination
4. Restricting to z-bins 2-4 and 6 would give a cleaner ACT sample

metrics.caveat: Naive subtraction shown for diagnostics; marginalized (Fisher) values are authoritative.

nodes.title: Deprojected likelihood: joint chi2 after systematic removal
nodes.body:
## What this tests

The fiducial likelihood evaluation (iteration 16) computed joint 6-bin chi2 against Planck 2018 theory using the full covariance matrix from PKL files. Template deprojection (iteration 21) showed per-bin amplitude shifts using diagonal Knox covariance. This analysis combines both: evaluating the joint likelihood on deprojected spectra with the full covariance.

The full covariance captures bandpower-to-bandpower correlations that the diagonal Knox formula ignores. For parameter inference, this is the definitive metric.

## Method

Three approaches compared:

**1. Original**: Joint 6-bin chi2 against Planck 2018 + NLA IA theory.

**2. Naive subtraction** (iteration 22): Subtract X_total from data vector, keep covariance unchanged. Known to inject template noise: d'^T C^{-1} d' = d^T C^{-1} d - 2(d^T C^{-1} X) + X^T C^{-1} X. The X^T C^{-1} X term dominates when templates are noisy, artificially inflating S/N.

**3. Template marginalization** (iteration 23, primary): Jointly fit signal amplitude A and per-systematic contamination amplitudes α_S. Model: d = A * theory + Σ_S α_S * X_S + noise. Best-fit via Fisher matrix: a_hat = (T^T C^{-1} T)^{-1} T^T C^{-1} d. The marginalized amplitude A and its uncertainty σ_A properly account for the contamination subspace without noise injection. Profile chi2 uses the residual after best-fit, with dof reduced by the number of fitted parameters (1 + n_systematic).

The marginalization resolves the S/N paradox: naive subtraction showed ACT S/N *increasing* after contamination removal (19→28σ for metacal), which was diagnosed as template noise injection. Marginalization gives the statistically correct detection significance.

## Results

| Metric | ACT lensmc | ACT metacal | SPT lensmc | SPT metacal |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| Original Â(IA) | 0.93±0.06 | 0.89±0.06 | 0.76±0.05 | 0.76±0.05 |
| Naive Â(IA) | 0.80±0.06 | 0.68±0.06 | 0.75±0.05 | 0.75±0.05 |
| **Marginalized Â(IA)** | **0.86±0.06** | **0.82±0.06** | **0.75±0.05** | **0.75±0.06** |
| Original χ²/dof | 0.88 | 0.96 | 1.88 | 1.67 |
| Naive χ²/dof | 2.44 | 5.51 | 1.85 | 1.69 |
| **Profile χ²/dof** | **0.75** | **0.82** | **1.61** | **1.54** |
| Marginalized S/N | 15σ | 13σ | 14σ | 14σ |
| Naive S/N | 22σ | 28σ | 21σ | 20σ |

## S/N paradox resolution

Naive subtraction showed S/N *increasing* after contamination removal (metacal ACT: 19→28σ). Template decomposition reveals the cause: d'^T C^{-1} d' = d^T C^{-1} d - 2(d^T C^{-1} X) + X^T C^{-1} X. For metacal×ACT: X^T C^{-1} X = 685 while 2·d^T C^{-1} X = 266 — the template noise term dominates. The "cleaned" data vector contains more power than the original because noisy templates inject quadratic noise into the chi2.

Template marginalization avoids this entirely — the Fisher matrix properly accounts for template noise, and the marginalized S/N (= A/σ_A) is the correct detection significance: ACT 15σ, SPT 14σ.

## Interpretation

**Contamination less severe than naive analysis suggested**: ACT lensmc Â moves from 0.93 to 0.86 (1.2σ from 1), not 0.80 (2.5σ). The profile chi2/dof *improves* from 0.88 to 0.75 — marginalization helps the fit. The previous "coincidental good fit" interpretation was an artifact of template noise injection.

**SPT deficit confirmed intrinsic**: Chi2/dof=1.6 persists after marginalization. The SPT normalization issue is CMB-side, not driven by galactic contamination.

**Metacal still more affected than lensmc**: ΔÂ = -0.07 (metacal) vs -0.07 (lensmc) for ACT after marginalization. Difference is smaller than with naive subtraction (Δ=-0.21 vs -0.13) — naive overcorrected metacal more because its templates are noisier.

## Caveats

1. **X_total is a lower bound**: C^{SS} noise-biased high → true contamination could be larger.
2. **Template amplitudes assumed known**: Marginalization fits a single scalar α per systematic (shared across ℓ-bins within each tomographic bin). The templates themselves (X_S) are treated as fixed — their measurement noise is not propagated. A fully Bayesian treatment would marginalize over template uncertainty, but this is a second-order correction.
3. **Flat prior on α_S**: No informative prior on contamination amplitudes. This is conservative — it allows the fit to absorb any fraction of the systematic signal.

**Script**: `workflow/scripts/evaluate_deprojected_likelihood.py`

nodes.title: Results table: paper-ready LaTeX compilation of all key numbers
nodes.body:
Compilation of all quantitative results into three paper-ready LaTeX tables. This is the tabular complement to the analysis summary figure — where the figure tells the story visually, the tables provide the definitive reference numbers.

## Table 1: Detection & theory (Tab. 1 in paper)

One row per (method × CMB) combination (4 total). Columns progress through the analysis chain: raw Knox S/N → marginalized S/N → amplitude fits at three theory stages (vanilla Planck 2018, NLA IA, template marginalized) → joint chi2/PTE. The table shows how each correction stage affects the result: IA shifts ACT Â from 0.84→0.93, marginalization further to 0.86. SPT remains at 0.76 regardless — the deficit is CMB-side.

Key numbers: ACT lensmc is the baseline (20σ raw, 15σ marginalized, Â=0.86±0.06 final, chi2/dof=0.75 PTE 0.98). SPT detects at comparable raw significance (21σ) but theory fails (chi2/dof=1.88, PTE≈0). metacal is consistent with lensmc across all quantities.

## Table 2: Per-bin breakdown (Tab. 2 in paper)

One row per tomographic bin (6 bins), lensmc only (metacal redundant at this detail level). Shows how detection significance rises from 5.5σ (bin 1, z~0.3) to 11.6σ (bin 6, z~1.5) — consistent with increasing lensing kernel overlap at higher source redshift. ACT amplitude fits are consistent with unity in all bins (PTE > 0.1); SPT fails in bins 1-3 and 6, with bin 2 showing the worst deficit (Â=0.74, chi2/dof=2.6). The per-bin pattern suggests the SPT deficit is not confined to a single redshift, arguing against n(z) miscalibration as the sole explanation.

## Table 3: Validation summary (Tab. 3 in paper)

Nine tests organized by type: 4 consistency checks (all pass), 3 null tests (one sim false alarm at 1/66 = expected rate), 1 systematic null (16/70 fail from galactic extinction + stellar density), and the bias budget. The catalog vs map row uses Pearson correlation (r=0.998) instead of PTE, since the comparison is between two pipeline implementations rather than a null hypothesis.

The bias budget entry (max 4.4σ stellar density, ACT) is the single most important validation concern — stellar density contamination correlates with both shear and CMB κ, producing a harmonic-space bias exceeding the signal in bin 1. Template marginalization handles this at the likelihood level.

## Technical notes

- Reads from `results/claims/{specName}/evidence.json` (both aggregate and individual files)
- Uses `ancient()` inputs to avoid DAG cascade
- Localrule (no SLURM needed — pure evidence compilation)
- Regenerable: `snakemake --rerun-triggers mtime generate_results_table`

nodes.title: ℓ-range robustness: amplitude stability under bandpower cuts
nodes.body:
Progressively includes bandpowers up to ℓ_max and fits the NLA IA theory amplitude Â, testing whether the measurement is robust across angular scales. This addresses a standard referee concern and constrains the origin of observed amplitude deficits.

## Method

For each (method, CMB, bin) combination, the script reads the 20 log-spaced bandpowers (ℓ = 44–2708) and fits Â(IA) using Knox diagonal covariance, progressively adding bandpowers from ℓ_max ≈ 105 (5 bp minimum) to full range. The fit follows the same weighted least-squares as `plot_theory_residual_analysis`: Â = Σ(d·t/σ²) / Σ(t²/σ²).

## Key findings

**ACT is scale-stable.** Worst max deviation from full-range Â is 1.5σ (bin 3, lensmc). Bins 4–6 are extremely stable (< 1σ). The ACT measurement is robust to ℓ-range selection.

**SPT deficit is scale-dependent.** SPT bins 1–3 show a pronounced downward trend: Â starts at ~1.5 when only low-ℓ bandpowers are included and drops to ~0.7 as high-ℓ data is added. This means the deficit is NOT a uniform calibration offset — high-ℓ bandpowers actively pull the amplitude below theory. Worst case: bin 2 (lensmc), max deviation 2.7σ, A range 0.74–2.55.

**SPT bins 4–6 more stable but still trend downward.** Max deviations 1.3–1.9σ. The high-z bins are less affected but not immune.

**Metacal shows similar patterns.** ACT somewhat less stable than lensmc (worst 1.9σ vs 1.5σ). SPT similar (worst 1.9σ vs 2.7σ — but metacal bin 2 has larger errors dampening the deviation metric).

## Interpretation

The scale dependence of the SPT deficit constrains its origin. Three hypotheses:

1. **SPT κ transfer function**: SPT lensing reconstruction has an effective ℓ_min ~ 500, and the response function falls off at high ℓ. If the Wiener-filtered κ map has residual transfer function effects not captured by our theory, they would appear as scale-dependent amplitude suppression.

2. **Missing baryonic feedback**: Baryonic effects suppress the matter power spectrum at k > 1 h/Mpc (ℓ > 500–1000 for these redshifts). This would cause Â to decrease as high-ℓ data is included — matching the observed trend. The effect should be similar for ACT and SPT, however, while the observed trend is much stronger for SPT.

3. **SPT noise bias leakage**: N0/N1 biases formally cancel in cross-correlation, but higher-order terms (N3/2, trispectrum) can leak. These grow at high ℓ and would suppress the cross-spectrum.

The fact that ACT does NOT show this trend favors explanation 1 or 3 (CMB-side, SPT-specific) over explanation 2 (universal physics). See also `spt-deficit-bin-level-structure-de2540dd` for the complementary per-bin analysis.
